category.label: INTERVIEWS
category.description: Investment wisdom extracted from legendary investors — the frameworks, principles, and mental models that have stood the test of time.

entries.title: Warren Buffett — Circle of Competence
entries.subtitle: Invest only in what you understand deeply: moats, compounding, and why temperament beats intellect.

rules_of_thumb.label: Circle of competence
rules_of_thumb.value: Only invest in what you can explain simply
rules_of_thumb.note: If you can't describe the business clearly, you don't understand it well enough to own it

rules_of_thumb.label: Moat test
rules_of_thumb.value: Will this advantage hold for 10 years?
rules_of_thumb.note: Brand, switching costs, network effects, low-cost production — name the moat specifically

rules_of_thumb.label: 20-punch-card rule
rules_of_thumb.value: Imagine only 20 investments in a lifetime
rules_of_thumb.note: Forces genuine conviction over trading activity

rules_of_thumb.label: Time on compounding
rules_of_thumb.value: Never interrupt unnecessarily
rules_of_thumb.note: Tax cost of selling a winner is real; pre-tax compounding is a structural advantage

pitfalls: Buffett doesn't just buy cheap stocks — he pays fair prices for exceptional businesses. The moat quality matters more than the P/E multiple.
Confusing using a product with understanding the business — consumer familiarity is the start of research, not the end.
Over-diversification is protection against ignorance, not a virtue — if you genuinely understand 5 businesses, concentrating in them beats 50 you don't.

entries.title: Charlie Munger — Latticework of Mental Models
entries.subtitle: Multi-disciplinary thinking, inversion, and systematically avoiding stupidity over trying to be brilliant.

rules_of_thumb.label: Inversion first
rules_of_thumb.value: Find all the ways to fail before finding the ways to succeed
rules_of_thumb.note: 'Tell me where I'm going to die and I'll never go there' — the most reliable path to success is eliminating failure modes

rules_of_thumb.label: Bias checklist
rules_of_thumb.value: Run your reasoning against 25 cognitive biases
rules_of_thumb.note: Confirmation, social proof, authority, commitment, incentive-caused bias are the most dangerous in investing

rules_of_thumb.label: Aggressive patience
rules_of_thumb.value: Act decisively when you have a clear edge, nothing otherwise
rules_of_thumb.note: Most activity reduces returns through taxes, friction, and errors

rules_of_thumb.label: Destroy your best ideas
rules_of_thumb.value: Work as hard to find the bear case as the bull case
rules_of_thumb.note: If you can't construct a compelling case against your thesis, you haven't thought hard enough

pitfalls: Mental models is about cross-disciplinary frameworks applied to specific situations — being generally well-read is necessary but not sufficient.
Inversion is a stress-testing tool, not a pessimistic worldview — Munger was optimistic about human progress while ruthless about specific investment risk.
Updating beliefs when evidence changes is a virtue in Munger's framework — he viewed slow belief-updating as commitment bias, one of the most damaging flaws.

external_links.title: A Lesson on Elementary, Worldly Wisdom (1994 speech)

entries.title: Ray Dalio — Debt Cycles and the All-Weather Portfolio
entries.subtitle: Long-term debt cycles, risk parity portfolio construction, and radical transparency in decision-making.

rules_of_thumb.label: All-Weather four environments
rules_of_thumb.value: Rising/falling growth × rising/falling inflation
rules_of_thumb.note: Gold, long bonds, commodities, equities perform in different regimes — balance the risk exposure across all four

rules_of_thumb.label: Risk parity vs. 60/40
rules_of_thumb.value: 60/40 has 90%+ of risk in equities
rules_of_thumb.note: Dollar diversification is not risk diversification — measure volatility contribution, not dollar weight

rules_of_thumb.label: Debt cycle template
rules_of_thumb.value: 75–100 year long cycles, 5–8 year short cycles
rules_of_thumb.note: Most crises are variations of the same deleveraging pattern — understanding the template helps contextualize current conditions

rules_of_thumb.label: Believability weighting
rules_of_thumb.value: Weight views by track record and relevant expertise
rules_of_thumb.note: Not all opinions are equal — a decision system should reflect this explicitly

pitfalls: All-Weather in unlevered form is heavily bonds and gold — it weathers all cycles but can lag significantly during equity bull markets.
The debt cycle framework is structural, not a timing tool — Dalio has been wrong on near-term predictions despite being right on structural dynamics.
Radical transparency is a specific organizational adaptation, not a universally applicable management philosophy — it requires enormous institutional commitment.

external_links.title: How the Economic Machine Works (30-min video)

entries.title: Peter Lynch — Tenbaggers and Growth at a Reasonable Price
entries.subtitle: Invest in what you observe, use PEG ratio, hold through volatility, and know when the story changes.

rules_of_thumb.label: PEG ratio fair value
rules_of_thumb.value: PEG = 1.0
rules_of_thumb.note: P/E divided by EPS growth rate — below 1 is potentially cheap, above 2 is expensive relative to growth

rules_of_thumb.label: Two-minute thesis test
rules_of_thumb.value: Can you explain why you own it in 2 minutes?
rules_of_thumb.note: If not, you don't understand the investment well enough to hold through adversity

rules_of_thumb.label: Tenbagger math
rules_of_thumb.value: One 10× in 10 positions = 100% portfolio gain
rules_of_thumb.note: Justifies holding growth positions through normal volatility instead of selling at 50–100%

rules_of_thumb.label: Sell signal
rules_of_thumb.value: The thesis, not the price
rules_of_thumb.note: Sell when the story changes materially, not because the stock is down or you have a profit

pitfalls: Invest in what you know means understanding the business economics — not just using the product. Consumer familiarity is the starting point, not the investment thesis.
PEG ratio breaks down for cyclical businesses (earnings at cycle peak inflate the denominator), very early-stage companies, and asset-heavy businesses.
Lynch ran a large fund by necessity; he argued individuals with 8–12 positions will outperform those with 100+ because depth of research matters more than breadth.

entries.title: Jim Simons — The Medallion Fund and Quantitative Investing
entries.subtitle: Signal over noise, capacity constraints, honest backtesting, and why organizational structure beats individual genius.

rules_of_thumb.label: Signal validation requirement
rules_of_thumb.value: Plausible reason + statistical significance + out-of-sample test
rules_of_thumb.note: All three must hold — a signal that only passes two is likely noise

rules_of_thumb.label: Capacity discipline
rules_of_thumb.value: Know the capacity of your strategy
rules_of_thumb.note: As capital deployed increases, market impact degrades the edge — Medallion stayed small deliberately

rules_of_thumb.label: Diversification at signal level
rules_of_thumb.value: Many uncorrelated signals, none dominant
rules_of_thumb.note: Portfolio construction applies to signals as well as positions — no single signal should be capable of being fatal

rules_of_thumb.label: Transaction costs first
rules_of_thumb.value: Model full costs before committing to a strategy
rules_of_thumb.note: Commissions + bid-ask spread + market impact + financing — most strategies that look good gross net to zero

pitfalls: Overfitting is the primary failure mode in quantitative investing — patterns that worked historically are often noise. Out-of-sample testing is mandatory, not optional.
Medallion required world-class mathematicians, vast proprietary datasets, and 35 years of iteration — the principles are accessible but replication is not.
Quant does not mean high-frequency — Simons's approach spans multiple time horizons including days to weeks. Systematic trading is the defining characteristic, not speed.

external_links.title: The Man Who Solved the Market (book)
Jim Simons: The mathematician who cracked Wall Street (TED Talk)

entries.title: George Soros — Theory of Reflexivity
entries.subtitle: Markets are always wrong, feedback loops drive booms and busts, and asymmetric macro bets exploit late-stage cycles.

rules_of_thumb.label: Reflexivity definition
rules_of_thumb.value: Prices affect fundamentals; fundamentals affect prices — feedback, not one-way causation
rules_of_thumb.note: Rising asset prices improve collateral → more lending → higher prices; this self-reinforcing loop is reflexivity

rules_of_thumb.label: Boom-bust arc
rules_of_thumb.value: Real trend → misperception amplifies → recognition gap → reversal → overshoot
rules_of_thumb.note: The arc is patterned even if timing is unpredictable — positioning against late-stage reflexive cycles is the macro trade

rules_of_thumb.label: Hypothesis testing
rules_of_thumb.value: Make a provisional bet, use market reaction as feedback
rules_of_thumb.note: If the market behaves as the thesis predicts, add; if it doesn't, exit and reassess

rules_of_thumb.label: Asymmetry requirement
rules_of_thumb.value: Limited loss if wrong, large gain if right
rules_of_thumb.note: The best reflexivity trades have structurally capped downside — like the pound peg where the cost of defending the peg rose nonlinearly

pitfalls: Reflexivity doesn't mean markets are random — it means they have predictable feedback patterns. The unpredictability is in timing, not in whether the cycle exists.
Soros didn't break the Bank of England alone — he was the largest participant in a trade dozens of macro funds were running. The fundamental imbalance was the cause.
Reflexivity amplifies genuine trends; it doesn't create value from nothing. The most powerful cycles start from a real underlying economic development.

external_links.title: Fallibility, Reflexivity, and the Human Uncertainty Principle (lecture)

entries.title: Howard Marks — Second-Level Thinking and Market Cycles
entries.subtitle: Risk is not volatility, what is priced in matters more than what is true, and the cycle tells you when to be aggressive.

rules_of_thumb.label: Second-level thinking test
rules_of_thumb.value: Not 'is this good?' but 'is this better or worse than what's priced in?'
rules_of_thumb.note: Consensus knowledge is already in the price — your edge requires being different AND correct

rules_of_thumb.label: Risk proxy indicators
rules_of_thumb.value: Tight spreads + low VIX + aggressive deal terms + 'everyone knows' narratives
rules_of_thumb.note: When these cluster, risk is high even if recent volatility is low

rules_of_thumb.label: Cycle positioning
rules_of_thumb.value: More aggressive when pessimism is widespread and prices are depressed
rules_of_thumb.note: The contrarian move is buying during fear as often as selling during greed

rules_of_thumb.label: Downside control first
rules_of_thumb.value: 'If we avoid the losers, the winners take care of themselves'
rules_of_thumb.note: Expected return is irrelevant if the distribution of outcomes includes permanent capital loss

pitfalls: Marks is not a perma-bear — his framework calls for maximum aggressiveness when prices are depressed and pessimism is widespread. Misreading this leads to sitting out bull markets.

Second-level thinking does not mean being contrarian for its own sake — sometimes consensus is right. The requirement is independent analysis, which often but not always leads to contrarian conclusions.
Marks explicitly states he cannot predict when markets will peak — his framework identifies elevated risk levels, not correction timing.

entries.title: Benjamin Graham — Margin of Safety and Mr. Market
entries.subtitle: Buy at a significant discount to intrinsic value; treat market prices as opportunities, not signals of truth.

rules_of_thumb.label: Margin of safety
rules_of_thumb.value: Buy significantly below conservative intrinsic value estimate
rules_of_thumb.note: The more uncertain the valuation, the larger the required discount — 20–30% for stable businesses, 40–50%+ for uncertain ones

rules_of_thumb.label: Mr. Market test
rules_of_thumb.value: Is Mr. Market offering this below what it's worth?
rules_of_thumb.note: Treat market prices as input to your decision when they're attractive, not as signals of what's true

rules_of_thumb.label: Net-net screen
rules_of_thumb.value: Current assets − all liabilities > market cap
rules_of_thumb.note: The most extreme form of value investing — buying below liquidation value of liquid assets alone

rules_of_thumb.label: Intrinsic value is a range
rules_of_thumb.value: Use multiple methods (earnings power, book value, replacement cost)
rules_of_thumb.note: Convergence of multiple conservative methods increases confidence; divergence signals uncertainty

pitfalls: Graham evolved beyond net-net deep value later in life — buying companies simply because they're statistically cheap doesn't work if the business quality is genuinely poor.

Value investing appears 'dead' in any period when growth stocks massively outperform; the principle (buy below intrinsic value) is regime-independent even when cheap stocks are in a long cycle of underperformance.
The Intelligent Investor's specific stock examples are dated; the Mr. Market allegory and margin of safety concept are as applicable now as in 1949.

external_links.title: The Intelligent Investor (book — updated with commentary by Jason Zweig)
